---
title: "Research"
page-layout: article
---

# Making hidden states visible

My work asks how we infer hidden states from noisy, high-dimensional evidence.

Hunger, satiety, impulsivity, attention, social influence, and model capability are not directly visible. We infer them from behavior, physiology, neural activity, molecular state, and context. I build experiments, models, and visual representations that make those inferences easier to compare, interpret, and trust.

## Three directions

**Internal states and neuromodulation** — serotonin, norepinephrine, feeding, satiety, attention, and decision-making. → [Neuroscience](neuroscience.html)

**Statistical and visual tools** — effect sizes, uncertainty, phenovectors, estimation graphics, and high-dimensional visualization. → [Open Source Tools](statistics.html)

**AI-assisted scientific inference** — LLM agents, rubric-based critique, manuscript evaluation, and reasoning without simple ground truth. → [Writing](blog/index.html)

## Current direction

Across animals, patients, users, robots, and AI agents, the problem is often the same: infer hidden state from noisy evidence, represent it faithfully, and decide what can be trusted.
